# PyAnsys Tools Versioning

{{ pyansys }} {{ python }} {{ pypi }} {{ GH-CI }} {{ codecov }} {{ MIT }} {{ black }} {{ pre-commit }}

Utilities for backwards and forwards server support.

## How to install

At least two installation modes are provided: user and developer.

### For users

In order to install PyAnsys Tools Versioning, make sure you
have the latest version of [pip]. To do so, run:

```bash
python -m pip install -U pip
```

Then, you can simply execute:

```bash
python -m pip install pyansys-tools-versioning
```

### For developers

Installing PyAnsys Tools Versioning in developer mode allows
you to modify the source and enhance it.

Before contributing to the project, please refer to the [PyAnsys Developer's guide]. You will
need to follow these steps:

1. Start by cloning this repository:

   > ```bash
   > git clone https://github.com/ansys/pyansys-tools-versioning
   > ```

2. Create a fresh-clean Python environment and activate it:

   > ```bash
   > # Create a virtual environment
   > python -m venv .venv
   >
   > # Activate it in a POSIX system
   > source .venv/bin/activate
   >
   > # Activate it in Windows CMD environment
   > .venv\Scripts\activate.bat
   >
   > # Activate it in Windows Powershell
   > .venv\Scripts\Activate.ps1
   > ```

3. Make sure you have the latest required build system and doc, testing, and CI tools:

   > ```bash
   > python -m pip install -U pip flit tox
   > python -m pip install -r requirements/requirements_build.txt
   > python -m pip install -r requirements/requirements_doc.txt
   > python -m pip install -r requirements/requirements_tests.txt
   > ```

4. Install the project in editable mode:

   > ```bash
   > python -m pip install --editable pyansys-tools-versioning
   > ```
   >
   > 5. Finally, verify your development installation by running:
   >
   > ```bash
   > tox
   > ```

## How to testing

This project takes advantage of [tox]. This tool allows to automate common
development tasks (similar to Makefile) but it is oriented towards Python
development.

### Using tox

As Makefile has rules, [tox] has environments. In fact, the tool creates its
own virtual environment so anything being tested is isolated from the project in
order to guarantee project's integrity. The following environments commands are provided:

- **tox -e style**: will check for coding style quality.
- **tox -e py**: checks for unit tests.
- **tox -e py-coverage**: checks for unit testing and code coverage.
- **tox -e doc**: checks for documentation building process.

### Raw testing

If required, you can always call the style commands ([black], [isort],
[flake8]...) or unit testing ones ([pytest]) from the command line. However,
this does not guarantee that your project is being tested in an isolated
environment, which is the reason why tools like [tox] exist.

### A note on pre-commit

The style checks take advantage of [pre-commit]. Developers are not forced but
encouraged to install this tool via:

```bash
python -m pip install pre-commit && pre-commit install
```

## Documentation

For building documentation, you can either run the usual rules provided in the
[Sphinx] Makefile, such us:

```bash
make -C doc/ html && your_browser_name doc/html/index.html
```

However, the recommended way of checking documentation integrity is using:

```bash
tox -e doc && your_browser_name .tox/doc_out/index.html
```

## Distributing

If you would like to create either source or wheel files, start by installing
the building requirements and then executing the build module:

```bash
python -m pip install -r requirements/requirements_build.txt
python -m build
python -m twine check dist/*
```

% LINKS AND REFERENCES

[black]: https://github.com/psf/black
[flake8]: https://flake8.pycqa.org/en/latest/
[isort]: https://github.com/PyCQA/isort
[pip]: https://pypi.org/project/pip/
[pre-commit]: https://pre-commit.com/
[pyansys developer's guide]: https://dev.docs.pyansys.com/
[pytest]: https://docs.pytest.org/en/stable/
[sphinx]: https://www.sphinx-doc.org/en/master/
[tox]: https://tox.wiki/

functions.name: ansys.tools.versioning.utils.sanitize_version_string
documentation:
Sanitize a version string number by adding additional zeros.

# Parameters

version_string

: A string representing a semantic version.

# Returns

str

: A string representing a semantic version.

# Examples

```pycon
>>> sanitize_version_string("0")
'0.0.0'
>>> sanitize_version_string("1.2")
'1.2.0'
>>> sanitize_version_string("0.3.4")
'0.3.4'
```

functions.name: ansys.tools.versioning.utils.sanitize_version_tuple
documentation:
Sanitize a version number by adding additional zeros.

# Parameters

version_tuple

: A tuple representing a semantic version.

# Returns

tuple

: A tuple representing a semantic version.

# Examples

```pycon
>>> sanitize_version_tuple((0,))
(0, 0, 0)
>>> sanitize_version_tuple((1, 2))
(1, 2, 0)
>>> sanitize_version_tuple((0, 3, 4))
(0, 3, 4)
```

functions.name: ansys.tools.versioning.utils.version_string_as_tuple
documentation:
Convert a semantic version string into a tuple.

# Parameters

version_string

: A string representing a semantic version.

# Returns

version_tuple

: A tuple representing a semantic version.

# Examples

```pycon
>>> version_string_as_tuple("0.3.4")
(0, 3, 4)
>>> version_string_as_tuple("1.2")
(1, 2, 0)
```

functions.name: ansys.tools.versioning.utils.version_tuple_as_string
documentation:
Convert a semantic version tuple into a string.

# Parameters

version_tuple

: A tuple representing a semantic version.

# Returns

str

: A string representing a semantic version.

# Examples

```pycon
>>> version_tuple_as_string((0, 3, 4))
'0.3.4'
>>> version_tuple_as_string((1, 2))
'1.2.0'
```

functions.name: ansys.tools.versioning.utils.server_meets_version
documentation:
Check if server meets the required version.

# Parameters

server_version

: A string or tuple representing the server version.
  If it is an object different from the previous ones, it must have a '\_server_version' attribute.

required_version

: A string or tuple representing the version to be meet.

# Returns

bool

: `True` if server version meets required version, `False` if not.

# Raises

ValueError

: If the 'server_version' object does not have '\_server_version' attribute.

# Examples

```pycon
>>> server_version, required_version = "1.2.0", "1.3.0"
>>> server_meets_version(server_version, required_version)
False
>>> server_version, required_version = (1, 2, 0), (1, 3, 0)
>>> server_meets_version(server_version, required_version)
False
>>> server_version, required_version = "2.3.0", "1.3.0"
>>> server_meets_version(server_version, required_version)
True
>>> server_version, required_version = (2, 3, 0), (1, 3, 0)
>>> server_meets_version(server_version, required_version)
True
>>> server_version, required_version = (0, 0, 0), (0, 0, 0)
>>> server_meets_version(server_version, required_version)
True
>>> class MyServer:
        def __init__(self):
            self._server_version = "1.2.0"
>>> server = MyServer()
>>> server_version, required_version = server, "1.3.0"
>>> server_meets_version(server, required_version)
```

functions.name: ansys.tools.versioning.utils.requires_version
documentation:
Ensure the method called matches a certain version.

# Parameters

version

: A string or tuple representing the minimum required version.

VERSION_MAP

: A dictionary relating server version and ANSYS unified install version.

# Raises

AttributeError

: Decorated class method requires `_server_version` attribute.

VersionError

: Decorated class method is not supported by server version.

classes.name: ansys.tools.versioning.utils.VersionMeta

methods.name: ansys.tools.versioning.utils.VersionMeta.__le__
documentation:
Less equal.

If compared against a string which contains 'dev' it will always evaluate to True.
If compared against an int, it will perform a classic 'less equal' operation.

methods.name: ansys.tools.versioning.utils.VersionMeta.__lt__
documentation:
Less than.

If compared against a string which contains 'dev' it will always evaluate to True.
If compared against an int, it will perform a classic 'less than' operation.

methods.name: ansys.tools.versioning.utils.VersionMeta.__ge__
documentation:
Greater equal.

If compared against a string which contains 'dev' it will always evaluate to False.
If compared against an int, it will perform a classic 'greater equal' operation.

methods.name: ansys.tools.versioning.utils.VersionMeta.__gt__
documentation:
Greater than.

If compared against a string which contains 'dev' it will always evaluate to False.
If compared against an int, it will perform a classic 'greater than' operation.

methods.name: ansys.tools.versioning.utils.VersionMeta.__eq__
documentation:
Equal method.

If compared against a string which contains 'dev' it will always evaluate to False.
If compared against an int, it will perform a classic 'equal' operation.

methods.name: ansys.tools.versioning.utils.VersionMeta.__ne__
documentation:
Not equal.

If compared against a string which contains 'dev' it will always evaluate to not
'equal' operation (True). If compared against an int, it will perform a classic 'not equal' operation.

methods.name: ansys.tools.versioning.utils.VersionMeta.__hash__
documentation: Call the underlying __hash__ method.

Metaclass for version comparison.

Implements modification to magic methods.

classes.name: ansys.tools.versioning.utils.SemanticVersion

methods.name: ansys.tools.versioning.utils.SemanticVersion.__new__
documentation:
Construct class.

Parameters
----------
cls : type
    Class type
__iterable : Iterable[str, int], optional
    Iterable with major, minor and patch numbers as str or int, by default None.
major : VersionNumber, optional
    Major version digit, by default None.
minor : VersionNumber, optional
    Minor version digit, by default None.
patch : VersionNumber, optional
    Patch version digit, by default None.

Returns
-------
myint, mystr
    Depending on the input, the output will be a myint or a mystr class.

methods.name: ansys.tools.versioning.utils.SemanticVersion.major
documentation: Return major version number.

methods.name: ansys.tools.versioning.utils.SemanticVersion.minor
documentation: Return minor version number.

methods.name: ansys.tools.versioning.utils.SemanticVersion.patch
documentation: Return patch version number.

methods.name: ansys.tools.versioning.utils.SemanticVersion.as_string
documentation: Return the version as string.

methods.name: ansys.tools.versioning.utils.SemanticVersion.as_tuple
documentation: Return the version as tuple.

methods.name: ansys.tools.versioning.utils.SemanticVersion.as_list
documentation: Return the version as list.

methods.name: ansys.tools.versioning.utils.SemanticVersion.as_dict
documentation: Return the version as dict.

Class for semantic versioning.

It is a subclass of tuple and can be instantiated from a string or a tuple.

You can use 'dev' in the patch version, but nowhere else.

classes.name: ansys.tools.versioning.utils.mystr
documentation: Custom class to hold strings for versioning.

classes.name: ansys.tools.versioning.utils.myint
documentation: Custom class to hold integers for versioning.

classes.name: ansys.tools.versioning.utils.VersionNumber

methods.name: ansys.tools.versioning.utils.VersionNumber.__new__
documentation:
Create and return a new object.

Parameters
----------
value : str or int
    Version value to be stored.

Returns
-------
mystr, myint
   Subclass of str or int depending on value.

Class for version comparison.

This class can be instantiated from a string or an integer.
The constructor will choose the corresponding class.

Any combination of 'dev' and integers will be considered as a string.
'dev' is considered as the highest version number possible.

# Examples

```pycon
>>> from ansys.tools.versioning.utils import VersionNumber
>>> VersionNumber(1)
1
>>> VersionNumber("dev")
'dev'
>>> VersionNumber(1) <= VersionNumber("dev")
True
>>> VersionNumber(99999) >= VersionNumber("dev")
False
>>> VersionNumber("dev") == VersionNumber("dev1")
False
>>> VersionNumber("dev") != VersionNumber("dev1")
True
```
